vulnerability.hypothesis: In ScalarReplacement.cpp, the MSlots case (line 287) in IsObjectEscaped only asserts in DEBUG builds that MSlots consumers are StoreDynamicSlot/LoadDynamicSlot. In release builds, if an MSlots node is consumed by an unexpected opcode (e.g., through optimization passes rewriting the graph after escape analysis), the object would be incorrectly scalar-replaced while a raw slots pointer derived from it is still used, leading to use-after-free of the slots pointer since the object was never actually allocated.

trigger.sequence:
1. Create a constructor that allocates objects with both fixed and dynamic slots
2. In a hot loop, create the object, access its dynamic slots through various patterns
3. Try to get optimization passes (GVN, LICM) to transform MSlots consumers into unexpected opcodes
4. If scalar replacement proceeds despite an unexpected Slots consumer, the slots pointer becomes dangling
5. Accessing the dynamic slot through the dangling pointer triggers UAF
